children.name: Herpetotheres
children.description: A Neotropical monotypic genus of specialised snake-hunting falcon. The laughing falcon's oversized head and black mask give it an almost comical appearance, but its precision in decapitating venomous serpents is deadly serious.

children.name: Herpetotheres cachinnans
children.description: A specialised snake-eater of Neotropical forests, instantly recognised by its black face mask, buffy white head, and far-carrying laughter-like yelps given at dawn and dusk that may last five minutes. It decapitates venomous snakes with surgical precision before consuming them.

children.name: Micrastur
children.description: Secretive Neotropical forest falcons built for manoeuvring through dense jungle understorey — short rounded wings and a long tail let them weave through branches in pursuit of lizards, birds, and small mammals. Most species have bare facial skin and are heard far more often than seen.

children.name: Micrastur ruficollis
children.description: The most widespread forest falcon, ranging from Mexico to Argentina through almost every wooded habitat. Its barred underparts and short wings allow it to navigate dense cover in pursuit of lizards, birds, and large insects.

children.name: Micrastur plumbeus
children.description: A slate-grey forest falcon restricted to the Chocó lowlands of western Colombia and northwestern Ecuador. Its rapid, barking calls are a hallmark of intact lowland rainforest, though the species itself is seldom glimpsed.

children.name: Micrastur gilvicollis
children.description: Formerly considered a subspecies of the barred forest falcon, this Amazonian resident has a grey throat and finer barring below. It hunts in the dim understorey of terra firme and várzea forests, often near streams.

children.name: Micrastur mintoni
children.description: Described to science only in 2003, this recently recognised species is endemic to the southeastern Amazon rainforest in Brazil and Bolivia. It is nearly identical to the lined forest falcon but differs subtly in tail pattern and voice.

children.name: Micrastur mirandollei
children.description: A large, dark-backed forest falcon with a white throat and finely barred white underparts, found from Costa Rica through Amazonia. It preys on birds and mammals up to the size of a small tinamou, which it ambushes from a concealed perch.

children.name: Micrastur semitorquatus
children.description: The largest forest falcon, nearly the size of a peregrine, with a prominent white or buff collar and a long graduated tail. It is the most vocal Micrastur, its loud penetrating cries often mistaken for those of a potoo or a human whistle.

children.name: Micrastur buckleyi
children.description: A poorly known forest falcon of the upper Amazon slopes in Ecuador, Peru, and southern Colombia. It resembles a smaller collared forest falcon with a shorter tail and is one of the least-studied raptors in South America.

children.name: Spiziapteryx
children.description: The smallest member of the caracara lineage, this Argentine and Uruguayan endemic resembles a miniature falcon with cryptic grey, white, and black spotted plumage. It hunts insects and lizards from exposed perches in dry woodland and savanna.

children.name: Spiziapteryx circumcincta
children.description: A peculiar little raptor of the Chaco and Monte regions of Argentina, Bolivia, Paraguay, and Uruguay, with a falcon-like silhouette but caracara affinities. Its white-spotted wing coverts and pale rump flash conspicuously as it bounds through dry scrub in pursuit of grasshoppers.

children.name: Caracara
children.description: Large, long-legged caracaras of open country, instantly recognisable by their flat-crowned heads, heavy hooked bills, and bare orange-red facial skin. They stride across the ground like corvids, omnivorously scavenging and hunting in a manner unique among raptors.

children.name: Caracara cheriway
children.description: The northern representative of the crested caracara complex, ranging from the southern United States through Central America and Cuba into northern South America. It is an opportunistic omnivore that walks boldly along roadsides, flipping cow pats for dung beetles and gathering at carcasses.

children.name: Caracara plancus
children.description: The familiar carancho of the South American pampas and Patagonia, a large, untidy-looking raptor that walks the ground with a raven-like gait. It is a consummate generalist, feeding on carrion, nestlings, stranded fish, and even fruit.

children.name: Daptrius
children.description: Two distinctive Neotropical caracaras united by bare facial skin and a taste for wasp brood and fruit alongside carrion and small prey. They are bold, social, and among the noisiest raptors in the Amazon, often encountered in family groups.

children.name: Daptrius ater
children.description: An all-black caracara of Amazonian and Guianan river courses, distinguished by a patch of bare orange facial skin and a piercing scream that carries across the water. It is omnivorous, taking fish, frogs, carrion, and fruit, and has even been observed picking ticks from tapirs.

children.name: Daptrius americanus
children.description: A striking black-and-white caracara with unfeathered red facial skin that gives it a vulturine appearance. It feeds primarily on wasp brood — raiding nests with astonishing immunity to stings — and is a cooperative breeder that maintains year-round territories.

children.name: Milvago
children.description: Smaller, paler caracaras of open lowland country, with a more slender build and a less imposing bill than their crested relatives. Both species are bold commensals of human habitation, often seen scavenging around farms, towns, and refuse dumps.

children.name: Milvago chimachima
children.description: A buffy-headed caracara of open and semi-open country from Costa Rica to northern Argentina, commonly seen perched on fence posts or scavenging along roads. It has expanded its range dramatically as deforestation has created new habitat, and is often nicknamed the 'chicken hawk' in Brazil.

children.name: Milvago chimango
children.description: The most abundant raptor of southern South America, a brownish, streaky caracara that flocks like a gull across the Argentine pampas, Chilean farmland, and Tierra del Fuego. It is a consummate opportunist, feeding on carrion, nestlings, insects, and refuse, and was introduced to Easter Island.

children.name: Phalcoboenus
children.description: Andean and Patagonian mountain caracaras — robust, long-legged birds of high-altitude grasslands and wind-scoured coasts with striking pied or barred plumage and bare yellow facial skin. They are bold and inquisitive, sometimes approaching hikers in search of scraps.

children.name: Phalcoboenus carunculatus
children.description: A black-and-white caracara of the high Andean páramo in Colombia and Ecuador, named for the fleshy orange wattle at the base of its bill. It patrols the bleak grassland in pairs or small groups, feeding on earthworms, insects, and carrion.

children.name: Phalcoboenus megalopterus
children.description: The most widespread Phalcoboenus, found from Peru to Patagonia in the high Andes at elevations of 2,000–5,000 metres. Its black body, white belly and tail, and bare orange face make it unmistakable against the short grass and rock of the puna.

children.name: Phalcoboenus albogularis
children.description: A dark-backed caracara with a pure white throat and breast, restricted to the southern Andes of Chile and Argentina. It inhabits Nothofagus forests and adjacent open country, often perching on fence posts and livestock, and was traditionally called the 'Güiña' by Mapuche people.

children.name: Phalcoboenus australis
children.description: The legendary 'Johnny Rook' of the Falkland Islands and Tierra del Fuego, a boldly barred raptor of sea cliffs and kelp beaches. It is one of the world's southernmost raptors, famously fearless of humans and specialised on scavenging seabird colonies during the breeding season.

children.name: Falco
children.description: The archetypal birds of prey — streamlined, pointed-winged, lightning-fast. The tomial tooth on the upper mandible severs prey's spinal cord on impact. Five species breed in Sweden regularly, from the hovering kestrel of roadsides to the peregrine reclaiming Stockholm's tower blocks.

children.name: Falco naumanni
children.description: A small, sociable kestrel that breeds colonially in old buildings and cliffs across the Mediterranean and Central Asia, migrating in large flocks to sub-Saharan Africa. Males have a rufous back and blue-grey head, lacking the dark spots of the common kestrel.

children.name: Falco tinnunculus
children.description: Sweden's familiar hovering falcon, hanging motionless on rapidly beating wings above roadside verges and field margins as it scans for voles. It can see ultraviolet light, allowing it to detect the urine trails of small rodents.

children.name: Falco newtoni
children.description: A small kestrel endemic to Madagascar and the Aldabra Atoll, where it occupies virtually every open habitat from rainforest edge to urban rooftops. It hunts insects and small vertebrates from low perches and hovers only briefly, unlike mainland kestrels.

children.name: Falco punctatus
children.description: A chunky, short-winged forest kestrel endemic to Mauritius, once reduced to just four individuals in 1974 — the rarest bird on Earth at the time. A legendary captive breeding programme led by the Durrell Wildlife Conservation Trust rescued it from extinction.

children.name: Falco araea
children.description: A diminutive chestnut-and-grey kestrel confined to the granitic Seychelles islands, where it hunts geckos, skinks, and insects from exposed perches in forest clearings and gardens. It was once down to a few hundred pairs but has staged a notable recovery.

children.name: Falco moluccensis
children.description: A heavily spotted kestrel endemic to Indonesia, found from Sumatra and Java east to Sulawesi and the Moluccas. It inhabits open woodland, farmland, and villages, where its loud kek-kek-kek call is a familiar sound.

children.name: Falco cenchroides
children.description: Australia's only kestrel, a pale rufous falcon named for the nankeen cloth of its back and wings. It hovers over paddocks and roadside verges across the entire continent, including Tasmania, and is a familiar sight even in urban areas.

children.name: Falco sparverius
children.description: The most colourful falcon in the world — males sport bright rufous backs, blue-grey wings, and a striking black-and-white head pattern. It is the smallest and most widespread North American falcon, often seen hovering above roadsides and power lines from Alaska to Tierra del Fuego.

children.name: Falco rupicoloides
children.description: A large, pale kestrel of the East and southern African savannas, distinguished from its smaller relatives by its pale eye, white underparts, and heavily barred back. It hunts from exposed perches rather than hovering, taking large insects, lizards, and small rodents.

children.name: Falco alopex
children.description: A large, uniformly rufous kestrel of the Sahel and East African arid zones, named for its fox-coloured plumage and elongated, pointed wings. It hunts in a buoyant, harrier-like flight over dry grassland and rocky hillsides, seldom hovering.

children.name: Falco ardosiaceus
children.description: A compact, slate-grey kestrel of open woodland and savanna across sub-Saharan Africa, with a distinctive pale grey head and dark eyes. It hunts from low perches, dropping onto grasshoppers, termites, lizards, and small birds, and tends to be crepuscular in habit.

children.name: Falco dickinsoni
children.description: A dark grey kestrel with a conspicuous white rump and barred tail, confined to miombo woodland and palm savanna in East and southern Africa. It specialises in hunting insects around baobab and palm trees, from which it takes its alternative name.

children.name: Falco zoniventris
children.description: An endemic Malagasy kestrel with a heavily barred grey belly, a bold white throat, and yellow eye-ring that give it a fierce expression. It hunts from perches in forest clearings and edges, preying on chameleons, geckos, and large insects.

children.name: Falco rupicolus
children.description: Recently split from the common kestrel, this southern African resident inhabits rocky outcrops, cliffs, and urban buildings from Angola and Zambia to the Cape. Males have a cleaner grey head and less spotting above than the migratory Eurasian birds.

children.name: Falco duboisi
children.description: A stocky, extinct kestrel endemic to Réunion Island, known only from subfossil bones discovered in cave deposits. It was driven to extinction by introduced predators and habitat loss in the late 17th century, before any scientific illustration was made.

children.name: Falco chicquera
children.description: A striking falcon with a rufous crown and nape, a bold black moustachial stripe, and blue-grey upperparts, found in two disjunct populations — sub-Saharan Africa and the Indian subcontinent. It hunts birds in level flight at high speed, often targeting parakeets and drongos.

children.name: Falco vespertinus
children.description: A graceful, sociable little falcon that breeds colonially in eastern Europe and appears in Sweden as a scarce but regular spring overshoot. Males are slate-grey with rust-red 'trousers' and red feet.

children.name: Falco amurensis
children.description: A long-distance migrant that breeds in eastern Asia and winters in southern Africa, making one of the most remarkable journeys of any raptor — a 30,000 km round trip across the Indian Ocean. Males are strikingly patterned with slate-grey body, white underwing coverts, and rufous thighs.

children.name: Falco eleonorae
children.description: A late-breeding Mediterranean falcon that nests on sea cliffs in August to synchronise chick-rearing with the autumn passerine migration, when the island skies fill with exhausted warblers and flycatchers. It has two colour morphs — a dark sooty and a pale rufous — and winters in Madagascar.

children.name: Falco concolor
children.description: A slender, uniformly soot-grey falcon of Red Sea islands and the Persian Gulf, which migrates to Madagascar and coastal East Africa for the non-breeding season. It hunts birds and insects on the wing at dawn and dusk, and its long, narrow wings give it a swift-like silhouette.

children.name: Falco columbarius
children.description: The smallest European falcon, a compact dashing hunter that pursues small birds in low fast flight across open country. Breeds on Swedish moorland and bogs and disperses to coasts and farmland in winter.

children.name: Falco rufigularis
children.description: A small, dark Neotropical falcon with a rufous throat and belly that hunts bats at dawn and dusk — a behaviour rare among raptors. It perches on exposed snags above the forest canopy and river edges, sallying out to catch its prey in mid-air.

children.name: Falco deiroleucus
children.description: A stocky, powerful falcon of Neotropical lowland forests, with a vivid rufous-orange breast and blue-grey upperparts reminiscent of a miniature peregrine. It hunts birds and bats in high-speed stoops from above the canopy and is rare and local throughout its range.

children.name: Falco femoralis
children.description: An elegant, long-tailed falcon of open grassland and savanna, with a distinctive 'moustached' face and a white breast band between dark blue-grey upperparts and rufous belly. It hunts low over the grass in a graceful, buoyant flight, often in pairs.

children.name: Falco subbuteo
children.description: A dashing, scythe-winged summer migrant that hawks dragonflies and hirundines in spectacular aerial pursuits, often over wetlands at dusk. It catches and eats much of its insect prey on the wing.

children.name: Falco cuvierii
children.description: A diminutive hobby of sub-Saharan African forests and woodlands, named after the French naturalist Frédéric Cuvier. It hunts dragonflies, swallows, and swifts in fast aerial pursuits above the canopy, and its presence is often revealed by its shrill, chattering call.

children.name: Falco severus
children.description: A small, dark hobby with a rufous belly, found from the eastern Himalayas through Southeast Asia to New Guinea and the Solomon Islands. It hunts swifts, swallows, and bats in high-speed chases over forest clearings and rivers, and its name reflects its fierce, determined hunting style.

children.name: Falco longipennis
children.description: Australia's counterpart to the Eurasian hobby, a slender, scythe-winged falcon that hunts small birds and dragonflies in rapid pursuit over woodland and wetlands. It is partly migratory in the south of the continent and has also colonised Timor and nearby islands.

children.name: Falco berigora
children.description: A large, broad-winged falcon with unusually long legs, found across Australia and New Guinea in a range of colour morphs from pale buff to dark chocolate. It hunts from exposed perches, dropping onto terrestrial prey, and is known for its sedentary, perching habits.

children.name: Falco novaeseelandiae
children.description: New Zealand's only endemic falcon, a powerful and aggressive predator of forest and mountain country that hunts birds up to the size of a tūī or a duck. It is famous for its spectacular aerial attacks on much larger birds, including harriers and even humans near the nest.

children.name: Falco hypoleucos
children.description: One of Australia's rarest and most elusive raptors, a pale, ghost-like falcon of the arid interior with fine grey barring above and white below. It hunts birds in fast, low flight over dry riverbeds and stony plains, and its population is thought to number only a few hundred pairs.

children.name: Falco subniger
children.description: A slender, uniformly sooty-black falcon of the Australian open plains, with long pointed wings and a narrow tail that give it a rakish silhouette. It hunts birds in level flight and from a soaring height, and is the only entirely black Australian raptor.

children.name: Falco biarmicus
children.description: A large, pale-crowned falcon of African savannas and Mediterranean cliffs, intermediate in build between the peregrine and the saker. It hunts birds in level flight rather than the vertical stoop of a peregrine, often in pairs that coordinate to flush prey.

children.name: Falco jugger
children.description: A handsome hierofalcon of the Indian subcontinent's dry plains and scrub, heavily streaked below with a white eyebrow and a rufous nape. It hunts birds, bats, and ground squirrels in low flight and was once widely used in falconry in the Mughal tradition.

children.name: Falco cherrug
children.description: A large, broad-winged falcon of open steppes and plains from Central Europe to Mongolia, prized for centuries by falconers across the Middle East and Central Asia. It hunts ground squirrels, hares, and large birds in a powerful, sustained chase rather than a vertical stoop.

children.name: Falco rusticolus
children.description: The largest falcon in the world, a powerful predator of the Swedish mountain tundra where it hunts ptarmigan and waterfowl in a fast, level chase rather than a stoop. Birds range from near-white to dark grey-brown.

children.name: Falco mexicanus
children.description: A pale, sandy-brown falcon of the arid American West, with dark 'armpit' patches contrasting against its white underparts. It hunts ground squirrels, birds, and insects in a fast, shallow glide over the prairies, and nests on cliff ledges in remote canyons.

children.name: Falco peregrinus
children.description: The fastest animal on Earth, reaching over 300 km/h in a hunting stoop on flying prey. After near-extinction from DDT, it has recovered strongly and now nests on cliffs, quarries, and city towers across Sweden.

children.name: Falco pelegrinoides
children.description: A pale desert falcon of North Africa and the Middle East, sometimes treated as a subspecies of the peregrine but distinguished by its rufous nape and slighter build. It hunts sandgrouse, doves, and pigeons in dramatic stoops over cliffs and wadis.

children.name: Falco fasciinucha
children.description: One of Africa's rarest falcons, confined to a handful of cliff sites in Kenya, Tanzania, Zimbabwe, and South Africa. It is a small, powerfully built falcon with a rufous nape and white face, resembling a miniature peregrine, and hunts swifts and swallows in fast aerial chases.
